one_line_takeaway: Inexact machine unlearning looks private only because it is benchmarked against weak population membership attacks; a per-example likelihood-ratio attack (U-LiRA) shows most methods still leak strongly on both vision and language models, and unlearning can even worsen privacy for data never asked to be forgotten.
key_finding: Per-example U-LiRA substantially outperforms the population baseline and shows unlearning is far less private than claimed. On vision, with no unlearning both attacks exceed 78% accuracy; the population baseline drops below 55% for the strongest methods (SCRUB, SPARSITY), suggesting near-perfect protection, but U-LiRA's advantage over random guessing is 2-3x larger than the baseline's, so meaningful leakage remains. On PaLM 2, U-LiRA accuracy never drops below 80% except when unlearning random strings, and both NegGrad and GradDesc fail to unlearn any samples when measured with U-LiRA. Unlearning also harms retain-set privacy: for several methods the attack is MORE successful after unlearning on a large fraction of retain examples (NegGrad+ worsens ~70% of the retain set; the two SOTA methods increase leakage on over half of non-forget points). Success is highly sensitive to when unlearning is stopped (over-unlearning re-exposes samples via extreme losses), and even a purpose-built U-LiRA-aware defense leaves U-LiRA accuracy above 75%.
core_contribution: The paper argues that the machine-unlearning literature has a 'false sense of privacy' because it evaluates inexact unlearning with weak, population-level membership inference attacks. Its own contribution is to formalize the membership inference game for unlearning (U-MIA), to categorize existing U-MIAs into weaker 'population' attacks (one attacker for all examples) versus stronger 'per-example' attacks (a dedicated attacker per example), and to introduce U-LiRA, an adaptation of Carlini et al.'s per-example LiRA to the unlearning setting in which the 'in' shadow distribution is built from models that trained on and then UNLEARNED the target example. Using U-LiRA the authors re-evaluate a suite of recent unlearning algorithms and show they leak far more membership signal than previously reported. They further surface two under-appreciated phenomena: (1) unlearning helps some forget-set examples while hurting others, and (2) unlearning can WORSEN the privacy of retain-set examples that were never requested for deletion (an 'onion effect' during unlearning). The paper is evaluative/critical rather than proposing a new defense; the U-LiRA-aware unlearning attempt is explicitly a negative result, not a solution.
threat_model: An adversary running the unlearning membership inference game: a model is trained on D_train, a forget set D_f is unlearned to produce theta_f, and the adversary, given a query point and the unlearned model, must decide whether the point was in training-then-unlearned (setting B) versus never trained on / retrained-from-scratch (setting A). Following Carlini et al.'s LiRA assumptions, the adversary knows the data distribution, the training algorithm A, and the unlearning algorithm U, and can train many shadow models (worst-case privacy adversary). Successful unlearning = the adversary cannot beat random guessing. The paper deliberately inherits these strong-adversary assumptions because inexact unlearning methods rarely specify a formal threat model.
limitations: Scope is limited to two settings (ResNet-18/CIFAR-10 class-5 forget sets and PaLM 2 fine-tuned on a small ~1,300-example dataset); the LLM study uses only NegGrad/GradDesc (SCRUB was excluded as not designed for generation) and small shadow counts (20 in/out). U-LiRA itself is not always optimal: it assumes Gaussian in/out logit distributions, and the authors show unlearning can reshape distributions into bimodal/skewed shapes where the Gaussian fit collapses to ~50-58% while a KDE-based test recovers 56-80% - so their own strong attack can under-report leakage. The analyses focus on average/worst-case leakage over sampled task datasets rather than adversarially chosen datasets. The work is diagnostic: it does not deliver a working inexact-unlearning defense, and its main constructive recommendation (adopt formal threat models and per-sample calibration / algorithmic guarantees) is aspirational.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (ResNet-18, trained on 25,000-example halves; forget set = 200 examples of class 5)
LIMA instruction-tuning dataset (1,000 examples) used to fine-tune PaLM 2 S (Bison)
300 handcrafted examples added to LIMA: three 100-example subsets named random (alphanumeric strings), PII, and poison (false/adversarial answers)
quantitative_results: With no unlearning, both the baseline U-MIA and U-LiRA exceed 78% membership-inference accuracy on CIFAR-10
Population baseline attack accuracy drops below 55% for the strongest methods SCRUB and SPARSITY, but U-LiRA's advantage over random guessing is 2-3x larger than the baseline's
256 ResNet-18 models trained; 256x40 = 10,240 unlearned models (5,120 shadow + 5,120 target); N=128 shadow models per example; ~73% CIFAR-10 test accuracy
On PaLM 2, U-LiRA accuracy never drops below 80% except when unlearning random data; both NegGrad and GradDesc fail to unlearn any samples under U-LiRA
PaLM 2 experiment used 2,400 models total (20 out x 20 in x 3 subsets x 2 unlearning methods); fine-tuned 15 epochs, lr 1e-5, weight decay 0.1, batch 64

NegGrad+ makes the attack more successful after unlearning on nearly 70% of retain-set examples; for the two SOTA methods (SCRUB [24], SPARSITY [21]) privacy leakage increases on over half of non-forget training points
The purpose-built 'U-LiRA-aware' unlearning algorithm still leaves U-LiRA accuracy above 75% at its optimal stopping point
Where U-LiRA's Gaussian assumption fails: bimodal toy example gives 58% (Gaussian) vs ~80% (KDE); a skewed long-tailed CIFAR-10 example gives 50.1% (Gaussian) vs 55.6% (KDE)
baselines_compared: Population U-MIA baseline of Kurmanji et al. (binary classifier trained on losses) - reported as the strongest population attack among those tested

Unlearning algorithms benchmarked: CF-k and EU-k (Goel et al.), NegGrad (gradient ascent on forget set), GradDesc (gradient descent on retain set), NegGrad+ (Kurmanji et al.), SPARSITY / l1-sparse MU (Jia et al.), SCRUB (Kurmanji et al.)
Retrain-from-scratch as the gold-standard 'ideal' unlearning reference; random guessing (50%) as the optimal-unlearning attack floor
